narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10 (well below the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 31 (compared to the typical 36.7), Year 1 school decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 2 (against 5.53), and full-time enrollment status—and calculated a 61.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates more likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 2, which signals weaker performance relative to peers and strongly raised concerns about your bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing the model's view that your early grades indicate ongoing challenges. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income level worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward success compared to lower-income peers, as it suggests access to resources that can aid preparation. These top three elements—Year 3 decile as the biggest driver, followed by Year 1 decile, with family income providing some offset—together tipped the scales toward rejection, even though your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%). Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your decile rankings are in the lower range, which is a common hurdle for those predicted to struggle on the bar. While your LSAT and GPA played roles, they weren't the dominant forces here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve future decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could significantly shift the model's prediction in your favor, opening doors to admission. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these targeted improvements.
